frameworks.name: recurrent neural network modeling
frameworks.one_line: trained RNNs as models of neural population dynamics in cognitive tasks

frameworks.name: inductive bias
frameworks.one_line: architectural choices bias which circuit solutions emerge during training

frameworks.name: model distillation
frameworks.one_line: distillation used to compare circuit solutions across activation functions

frameworks.name: fixed-point / dynamical-systems analysis
frameworks.one_line: fixed-point configurations characterize the learned circuit

core_question: Do single-unit activation functions in trained RNNs bias which circuit solutions emerge for cognitive tasks?
key_assumptions: trained RNNs are valid models of neural population computation
differences in single-unit nonlinearity are typically assumed architecturally inconsequential
circuit solutions can be compared via distilled student models
manipulations: single-unit activation function varied across trained RNNs
out-of-distribution test inputs probed beyond the training regime
key_findings: activation functions shape geometry of population trajectories
single-unit selectivity differs systematically with activation function
fixed-point configurations differ qualitatively across activation functions
RNNs with different activations show disparate out-of-distribution generalization
interpretations: activation functions act as strong inductive biases yielding qualitatively distinct circuit solutions
seemingly minor architectural choices change what the network is computing, not just how
